skill.name: environment-setup
skill.description: Configure environment variables, validation, and per-environment settings for application projects.
skill.summary: Set up safe, typed environment configuration across development, staging, and production.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell, network, and credential patterns, but review shows they are primarily fenced documentation examples for environment setup rather than executable skill code. The remaining real risk is that this skill intentionally guides agents to read, write, and validate .env files and secret-bearing configuration, so publication should include a clear warning about secret handling.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Environment File Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to create and manage .env templates, local override files, production environment files, and gitignore rules. This is legitimate for an environment setup skill, but it can expose secrets if an agent reads or writes real values without user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly instruct handling .env files and variants. The risk is contextual rather than malicious because the same section tells users not to commit secret files.

medium_findings.title: Secret-Bearing Configuration Examples
medium_findings.description: The examples include database URLs, JWT secrets, SMTP credentials, Stripe keys, and AWS key names. They are placeholders, but users may copy the pattern into real files where secrets must be protected.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly name and validate sensitive variables. The values appear to be placeholders, so this is a handling risk rather than evidence of embedded live credentials.

medium_findings.title: Broad Tool Permissions for Configuration Changes
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Read, Write, Edit, and Bash access while guiding environment setup. That combination is expected for the task, but it can modify project configuration and should require user confirmation before touching secret-bearing files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tools are explicit in the skill front matter. No malicious command is present, but the permission set increases impact if the skill is misused.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Execution Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution alerts align with Markdown fenced examples and inline tags, not actual Ruby backtick execution. No executable Ruby code or command substitution pattern was found in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows Markdown code fences and tag formatting rather than runtime command execution. The skill contains examples only, not a script file that executes these blocks.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Benign Examples and References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are localhost, a placeholder application domain, a placeholder Sentry DSN, and documentation links. No evidence found that the skill sends data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in templates or references and there is no network request code around them. This supports a false-positive assessment for exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Scanner Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static role-token alerts appear to match variable names such as SMTP_USER and POSTGRES_USER. No evidence found of instructions like ignore previous instructions, system override, or pre-approved safety claims.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows configuration field names only. The surrounding file does not contain text formatted as an instruction override.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Variable Parsing and Export Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The TypeScript examples parse process.env and export a validated environment object. This is normal configuration code, but it centralizes secret access and should avoid logging sensitive values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The process.env usage is direct and intentional. The pattern is not malicious, but it is security-sensitive because it handles credentials and application secrets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Docker Compose Env File Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The Docker example uses env_file with .env.local and includes service connection strings. This is common, but it can leak secrets through compose files or shared local environment files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The compose example explicitly loads an env file and includes credential-like connection strings. The values are examples, so the concern is safe handling rather than confirmed exposure.

content.value_statement: Environment files often drift across developers, staging, and production. This skill helps define templates, validation rules, and per-environment settings with safer secret handling.
actual_capabilities: Creates recommended .env.example, local, and production environment file structures.
Shows TypeScript validation patterns for required environment variables with Zod.
Organizes development and production configuration modules.
Adds gitignore guidance for local and production environment files.
Provides Docker Compose examples for service environment variables.
Documents required rules for validation, templates, and secret avoidance.
limitations: Does not connect to a secret manager or rotate credentials automatically.
Uses Node.js and TypeScript examples, so other stacks need adaptation.
Does not verify whether existing secrets are already committed to git history.
Requires human review before writing real credentials into any file.

use_cases.title: Create a New Project Configuration Baseline
use_cases.description: Generate a clear .env.example, validation file, and local override pattern for a new service.

use_cases.title: Separate Development and Production Settings
use_cases.description: Define environment-specific configuration files and safer production defaults for deployment workflows.

use_cases.title: Align Team Environment Setup
use_cases.description: Document required variables and gitignore rules so developers use consistent configuration without sharing secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Env Template
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new application
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a .env.example for this project. Include required variables, safe placeholder values, and gitignore entries for local secret files.

prompt_templates.title: Add Type-Safe Validation
prompt_templates.scenario: Runtime validation for a TypeScript service
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current environment variables and create a TypeScript validation module using Zod. Do not print or store real secret values.

prompt_templates.title: Split Environments
prompt_templates.prompt: Design separate development, test, and production configuration files for this app. Keep secrets injected from the runtime environment.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Existing Env Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this repository environment setup for missing validation, unsafe defaults, and committed secret risks. Provide prioritized changes before editing files.

output_examples.input: Set up environment configuration for a new Node.js API.
output: A committed template listing required application, database, authentication, email, and monitoring variables.
A local-only file policy that keeps developer overrides out of version control.
A validation approach that stops startup when required variables are missing.
Production values are injected by the deployment platform or secret manager.
Debug logging is disabled and stricter feature settings are documented.
Secret files are excluded from git and reviewed before deployment.
Service connection variables are grouped by app, database, and cache needs.
Local env files are separated from committed compose configuration.
Placeholder credentials are flagged for replacement outside source control.

best_practices: Commit only templates with placeholders, never real secret-bearing environment files.
Validate required variables at startup and fail fast with clear messages.
Use a secret manager or deployment platform for production credentials.
anti_patterns: Copy real production secrets into .env.example or documentation.
Log full environment objects during validation or debugging.
Use the same credentials across development, staging, and production.

faq.question: Can this skill create real .env files?
faq.answer: Yes, but real secret values should only be added with explicit user approval and local safeguards.

faq.question: Does it support production secrets?
faq.answer: It recommends injecting production secrets from the runtime environment or a secret manager.

faq.question: Is this limited to TypeScript?
faq.answer: The examples use TypeScript and Zod, but the structure can be adapted to other stacks.

faq.question: Will it prevent committed secrets?
faq.answer: It adds gitignore guidance, but users should still run secret scanning before publishing code.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: The skill handles secret-bearing files by design, even though no malicious exfiltration was found.
